title: The Rub占퐄y占퐐 of a Persian Kitten
content:
혻혻혻혻Wake! for the Golden Cat has put to flight
혻혻혻혻The Mouse of Darkness with his Paw of Light:
혻혻혻혻Which means, in Plain and simple every-day
혻혻혻혻Unoriental Speech--The Dawn is bright.




혻혻혻혻They say the Early Bird the Worm shall taste.
혻혻혻혻Then rise, O Kitten! Wherefore, sleeping, waste
혻혻혻혻The Fruits of Virtue? Quick! the Early Bird
혻혻혻혻Will soon be on the Flutter--O make haste!




혻혻혻혻The Early Bird has gone, and with him ta'en
혻혻혻혻The Early Worm--Alas! the Moral's plain,
혻혻혻혻O Senseless Worm! Thus, thus we are repaid
혻혻혻혻For Early Rising--I shall doze again.




혻혻혻혻The Mouse makes merry 'mid the Larder Shelves,
혻혻혻혻The Bird for Dinner in the Garden delves.
혻혻혻혻I often wonder what the creatures eat
혻혻혻혻One half so toothsome as they are Themselves.




혻혻혻혻And that Inverted Bowl of Skyblue Delf
혻혻혻혻That helpless lies upon the Pantry Shelf--
혻혻혻혻Lift not your eyes to It for help, for It
혻혻혻혻Is quite as empty as you are yourself.




혻혻혻혻The Ball no question makes of Ayes or Noes,
혻혻혻혻But right or left, as strikes the Kitten, goes;
혻혻혻혻Yet why, altho' I toss it Far Afield,
혻혻혻혻It still returneth--Goodness only knows!




혻혻혻혻A Secret Presence that my likeness feigns,
혻혻혻혻And yet, quicksilver-like, eludes my pains--
혻혻혻혻In vain I look for Him behind the glass;
혻혻혻혻He is not there, and yet He still remains.




혻혻혻혻What out of airy Nothing to invoke
혻혻혻혻A senseless Something to resist the stroke
혻혻혻혻Of unpermitted Paw--upon the pain
혻혻혻혻Of Everlasting Penalties--if broke.




혻혻혻혻I sometimes think the Pussy-Willows grey
혻혻혻혻Are Angel Kittens who have lost their way,
혻혻혻혻And every Bulrush on the river bank
혻혻혻혻A Cat-Tail from some lovely Cat astray.




혻혻혻혻Sometimes I think perchance that Allah may,
혻혻혻혻When he created Cats, have thrown away
혻혻혻혻The Tails He marred in making, and they grew
혻혻혻혻To Cat-Tails and to Pussy-Willows grey.




혻혻혻혻And lately, when I was not Feeling Fit,
혻혻혻혻Bereft alike of Piety and Wit,
혻혻혻혻There came an Angel Shape and offered me
혻혻혻혻A Fragrant Plant and bid me taste of it.




혻혻혻혻'Twas that reviving Herb, that Spicy Weed,
혻혻혻혻The Cat-Nip. Tho' 'tis good in time of need,
혻혻혻혻Ah, feed upon it lightly, for who knows
혻혻혻혻To what unlovely antics it may lead.




혻혻혻혻Strange--is it not?--that of the numbers who
혻혻혻혻Before me passed this Door of Darkness thro',
혻혻혻혻Not one returns thro' it again, altho'
혻혻혻혻Ofttimes I've waited here an hour or two.




혻혻혻혻'Tis but a Tent where takes his one Night's Rest
혻혻혻혻A Rodent to the Realms of Death address'd,
혻혻혻혻When Cook, arising, looks for him and then--
혻혻혻혻Baits, and prepares it for another Guest.




혻혻혻혻They say the Lion and the Lizard keep
혻혻혻혻The Courts where Jamsh占폻 gloried and drank deep.
혻혻혻혻The Lion is my cousin; I don't know
혻혻혻혻Who Jamsh占폻 is--nor shall it break my sleep.




혻혻혻혻Impotent glimpses of the Game displayed
혻혻혻혻Upon the Counter--temptingly arrayed;
혻혻혻혻Hither and thither moved or checked or weighed,
혻혻혻혻And one by one back in the Ice Chest laid.




혻혻혻혻What if the Sole could fling the Ice aside,
혻혻혻혻And with me to some Area's haven glide--
혻혻혻혻Were't not a Shame, were't not a shame for it
혻혻혻혻In this Cold Prison crippled to abide?




혻혻혻혻Some for the Glories of the Sole, and Some
혻혻혻혻Mew for the proper Bowl of Milk to come.
혻혻혻혻Ah, take the Fish and let your Credit go,
혻혻혻혻And plead the rumble of an empty Tum.




혻혻혻혻One thing is certain: tho' this Stolen Bite
혻혻혻혻Should be my last and Wrath consume me quite,
혻혻혻혻One taste of It within the Area caught
혻혻혻혻Better than at the Table lost outright.




혻혻혻혻Indeed, indeed Repentance oft before
혻혻혻혻I swore, but was I hungry when I swore?
혻혻혻혻And then and then came Cook--with Hose in hand--
혻혻혻혻And drowned my glory in a sorry pour.




혻혻혻혻What without asking hither harried whence,
혻혻혻혻And without asking whither harried hence--
혻혻혻혻O, many a taste of that forbidden Sole
혻혻혻혻Must down the memory of that Insolence.




혻혻혻혻Heaven, but the vision of a Flowing Bowl;
혻혻혻혻And Hell, the sizzle of a Frying Sole
혻혻혻혻Heard in the hungry Darkness, where Myself,
혻혻혻혻So rudely cast, must impotently roll.




혻혻혻혻The Vine has a tough Fibre which about
혻혻혻혻While clings my Being;--let the Canine Flout
혻혻혻혻Till his Bass Voice be pitched to such loud key
혻혻혻혻It shall unlock the door I mew without.




혻혻혻혻Up from the Basement to the Seventh Flat
혻혻혻혻I rose, and on the Crown of Fashion sat,
혻혻혻혻And many a Ball unravelled by the way--
혻혻혻혻But not the Master's angry Bawl of "Scat!"




혻혻혻혻Then to the Well of Wisdom I--and lo!
혻혻혻혻With my own Paw I wrought to make it flow,
혻혻혻혻And This was all the Harvest that I reaped:
혻혻혻혻We come like Kittens and like Cats we go.




혻혻혻혻Why be this Ink the Fount of Wit?--who dare
혻혻혻혻Blaspheme the glistening Pen-drink as a snare?
혻혻혻혻A Blessing?--I should spread it, should I not?
혻혻혻혻And if a Curse--why, then upset it!--there!




혻혻혻혻A moment's Halt, a momentary Taste
혻혻혻혻Of Bitter, and amid the Trickling Waste
혻혻혻혻I wrought strange shapes from M占퐃 to M占퐃i, yet
혻혻혻혻I know not what I wrote, nor why they chased.




혻혻혻혻Now I beyond the Pale am safely past.
혻혻혻혻O, but the long, long time their Rage shall last,
혻혻혻혻Which, tho' they call to supper, I shall heed
혻혻혻혻As a Stone Cat should heed a Pebble cast.




혻혻혻혻And that perverted Soul beneath the Sky
혻혻혻혻They call the Dog--Heed not his angry Cry;
혻혻혻혻Not all his Threats can make me budge one bit,
혻혻혻혻Nor all his Empty Bluster terrify.




혻혻혻혻They are no other than a moving Show
혻혻혻혻Of whirling Shadow Shapes that come and go
혻혻혻혻Me-ward thro' Moon illumined Darkness hurled,
혻혻혻혻In midnight, by the Lodgers in the Row.




혻혻혻혻Myself when young did eagerly frequent
혻혻혻혻The Backyard Fence and heard great Argument
혻혻혻혻About it, and About, yet evermore
혻혻혻혻Came out with Fewer Fur than in I went.




혻혻혻혻Ah, me! if you and I could but conspire
혻혻혻혻To grasp this Sorry Scheme of things entire,
혻혻혻혻Would we not shatter it to bits, and then
혻혻혻혻Enfold it nearer to our Heart's Desire?




혻혻혻혻Tho' Two and Two make Four by rule of line,
혻혻혻혻Or they make Twenty-two by Logic fine,
혻혻혻혻Of all the Figures one may fathom, I
혻혻혻혻Shall ne'er be floored by anything but Nine.




혻혻혻혻And fear not lest Existence shut the Door
혻혻혻혻On You and Me, to open it no more.
혻혻혻혻The Cream of Life from out your Bowl shall pour
혻혻혻혻Nine times--ere it lie broken on the Floor.




혻혻혻혻So, if the Fish you Steal--the Cream you drink--
혻혻혻혻Ends in what all begins and ends in, Think,
혻혻혻혻Unless the Stern Recorder points to Nine,
혻혻혻혻Tho' They would drown you--still you shall not sink.
meaning:
Exploring the themes of classic, Oliver Herford delivers a powerful performance in "The Rub占퐄y占퐐 of a Persian Kitten"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
